explicit_text: I have been working on a project that starts with Martin Luther King Jr., reflecting on his significance as I look back on my own life. Although my story begins earlier, King represents a pivotal figure in the civil rights movement that shaped the era I lived through. His legacy is a backdrop to my experiences growing up in the South and moving to Memphis.

explicit_text: My wife and I both grew up in Memphis, Tennessee, though I was not born there. When I was about fourteen, I moved to Memphis from Arkansas to pursue better schooling and work opportunities. Memphis was a bustling city compared to where I came from, with exciting neon signs and a vibrant black community centered around Beale Street, where everyone congregated.

explicit_text: I was born in Mississippi, specifically in a small town called Bentonia, about twenty miles from Jackson. My early years were spent in Mississippi, including Ruleville, where my family were sharecroppers. The atmosphere there was harsh and oppressive, with overseers enforcing strict control and violence against blacks, which eventually forced my family to leave.

My early years were spent there, including in a town where my family were sharecroppers. explicit_text: Bentonia, Mississippi, is where I was born and spent my earliest years. I remember coming into the town square with my father to sell watermelons when I was six or seven. Though many people I knew had moved away or passed on, some of the old stores and the railway station still stood when I visited recently, bringing back vivid memories of my childhood.

explicit_text: Jackson, Mississippi, was the nearest city to my hometown of Bentonia, about twenty miles away. While I didn’t live there, it was a point of reference for where Bentonia was located. Jackson represented a larger urban center compared to the rural areas where I grew up.
implicit_text: The nearest city to my hometown, about twenty miles away, served as a point of reference for where my small town was located. This city represented a larger urban center compared to the rural areas where I grew up.

explicit_text: Bentonia is located in Hinds County, Mississippi, which is where I was born. This county was part of the rural South where my family lived and worked as sharecroppers. The social and racial dynamics of Hinds County shaped much of my early life experience.

explicit_text: After leaving Bentonia, my family moved to Ruleville, Mississippi, when I was about eight or nine years old. We continued sharecropping there, and I vividly remember the oppressive overseer who rode around on a horse with a gun. It was in Ruleville that my father had a confrontation with the overseer and had to leave quickly, which led to our family’s escape from the area.

explicit_text: I was born in 1919, the year known as the Red Summer because of the widespread racial violence and riots that erupted across the country. I learned that these events happened in places like Texas, Chicago, Detroit, and Knoxville, Tennessee, as well as near Elaine, Arkansas. This violent atmosphere was part of the harsh realities black people faced during my early years.

This violent atmosphere was part of the harsh realities black people faced during my early years.

explicit_text: World War I ended just before I was born in 1919, and the aftermath of the war coincided with the Red Summer of racial violence. The social tensions following the war affected many black communities across the South and the nation, setting the stage for the struggles I witnessed growing up.

explicit_text: Texas was one of the states where significant racial riots occurred during the Red Summer of 1919. Although I did not live there, I was aware of the violence and bloodshed that took place in Texas, which was part of the broader pattern of racial unrest across the South and other parts of the country.

explicit_text: Elaine, Arkansas, was the site of a major racial riot during the Red Summer of 1919, just across the river from where I later lived in Arkansas. This event reflected the brutal racial oppression that black sharecroppers and farmers faced in the region. Though I was not there at the time, the memory of such violence was part of the atmosphere in which I grew up.

explicit_text: After leaving Mississippi, my family moved to Arkansas, where we lived in places like Helena, Forrest City, and Marked Tree during the early 1930s. Arkansas was different from Mississippi in some ways; for example, I experienced a more liberal overseer who encouraged schooling. However, racial tensions and violence were still very much a part of life in Arkansas as well.

explicit_text: Helena, Arkansas, was one of the towns we passed through after fleeing Mississippi. It was part of our journey as we moved to find safer and better opportunities. Although I didn’t stay long there, Helena marked a step in our migration northward within Arkansas.
implicit_text: One of the towns we passed through after fleeing Mississippi was a river city in eastern Arkansas. It was part of our journey as we moved to find safer and better opportunities. Although I didn’t stay long there, this town marked a step in our migration northward within the state.

explicit_text: Forrest City, Arkansas, was another stop on our way after leaving Mississippi. Like Helena, it was part of the path we took to escape the harsh conditions of sharecropping and racial violence. These towns were waypoints before settling in Marked Tree.

explicit_text: In Marked Tree, Arkansas, I lived and worked on a small farm in the early 1930s under a white overseer named Mr. Shaw who was much more liberal than those I knew in Mississippi. He worked alongside us in the fields and encouraged us to attend school, saying learning was important. This was a rare and positive experience compared to my earlier years.

explicit_text: While living in Arkansas, I had heard about the Southern Tenant Farmers Union, but it never really reached the area where I lived or affected my family directly. We lived on a farm and had a relatively peaceful relationship with some white neighbors, so the union’s activities were somewhat vague to us. It was more of a distant concept than a part of my daily life.

explicit_text: When I first arrived in Memphis, Beale Street was the center of the black community and a place where everyone congregated. It was exciting and vibrant, full of life and music, and it represented a stark contrast to the rural places I had come from. Beale Street was where I experienced the energy of city life as a young boy starting out.

explicit_text: After moving to Memphis, I attended LeMoyne College for a time while working. The college was part of my educational journey as I transitioned from rural life to the city. It represented an opportunity to continue learning beyond what was available in the rural schools I had attended before.
